questions.q:
Let A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and B = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}. Find:
a) A ∪ B
b) A ∩ B
c) A - B
d) B - A
questions.answer:
Given A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and B = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}.
a) A ∪ B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8}.
b) A ∩ B = {4, 5}.
c) A - B = {1, 2, 3}.
d) B - A = {6, 7, 8}.
The union of A and B is the set of elements which are in A or in B or in both.
The intersection of A and B is the set of elements which are in both A and B.
The difference of A and B is the set of elements which are in A but not in B.
marking_scheme: A ∪ B is calculated correctly (1 mark)
A ∩ B is calculated correctly (1 mark)
A - B is calculated correctly (1 mark)
B - A is calculated correctly (1 mark)
Explanation and definition of union, intersection and difference of sets (1 mark)

questions.q:
If n(A) = 20, n(B) = 30, and n(A ∪ B) = 40, find:
a) n(A ∩ B)
b) n(A' ∩ B')
questions.answer:
Given n(A) = 20, n(B) = 30, and n(A ∪ B) = 40.
a) We know that n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B).
n(A ∩ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∪ B)
n(A ∩ B) = 20 + 30 - 40 = 10.
b) We know that n(A' ∩ B') = n((A ∪ B)')
n(A' ∩ B') = n(U) - n(A ∪ B)
n(A' ∩ B') = 50 - 40 = 10.
n(A' ∩ B') = n(A' ∩ B') = 10.
marking_scheme: n(A ∩ B) is calculated correctly (1 mark)
n(A' ∩ B') is calculated correctly (1 mark)
Explanation and correct application of inclusion-exclusion principle (1 mark)
Correct calculation of n(A ∪ B) using inclusion-exclusion principle (1 mark)
Correct calculation of n(U) (1 mark)

questions.q:
If A = {x / x is a natural number and x ≤ 10}, B = {x / x is a natural number and x ≥ 5}, and C = {x / x is a natural number and x is even}. Find:
a) A ∩ B ∩ C
b) (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C)
questions.answer:
Given A = {x / x is a natural number and x ≤ 10}, B = {x / x is a natural number and x ≥ 5}, and C = {x / x is a natural number and x is even}.
a) A ∩ B ∩ C = {x / x is a natural number and x ≤ 10 and x ≥ 5 and x is even}.
A ∩ B ∩ C = {6, 8, 10}.
b) (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C) = {x / x is a natural number and x ≤ 10 and x ≥ 5 or x is a natural number and x ≤ 10 and x is even}.
(A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C) = {5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10}.
The intersection of A, B, and C is the set of elements which are in A, B, and C.
The union of (A ∩ B) and (A ∩ C) is the set of elements which are in (A ∩ B) or in (A ∩ C).
marking_scheme: A ∩ B ∩ C is calculated correctly (1 mark)
(A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C) is calculated correctly (1 mark)
Explanation and definition of intersection and union of sets (1 mark)
Correct identification of elements in A ∩ B ∩ C (1 mark)
Correct identification of elements in (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C) (1 mark)

questions.q:
If A and B are two sets such that n(A) = 50, n(B) = 60, and n(A ∩ B) = 20, find:
a) n(A ∪ B)
b) n(A' ∩ B')
questions.answer:
Given n(A) = 50, n(B) = 60, and n(A ∩ B) = 20.
a) We know that n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B).
n(A ∪ B) = 50 + 60 - 20 = 90.
b) We know that n(A' ∩ B') = n((A ∪ B)')
n(A' ∩ B') = n(U) - n(A ∪ B)
n(A' ∩ B') = 100 - 90 = 10.
n(A' ∩ B') = 10.
marking_scheme: n(A ∪ B) is calculated correctly (1 mark)
n(A' ∩ B') is calculated correctly (1 mark)
Explanation and correct application of inclusion-exclusion principle (1 mark)
Correct calculation of n(A ∪ B) using inclusion-exclusion principle (1 mark)
Correct calculation of n(U) (1 mark)
A × B is calculated correctly (1 mark)
B × A is calculated correctly (1 mark)
Explanation and definition of Cartesian product of sets (1 mark)
Correct identification of elements in A × B (1 mark)
Correct identification of elements in B × A (1 mark)
